refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making worksite geometry and features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent DP selection and IM packaging.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema/format, contains spatial coordinates and feature classifications, and is accessible to downstream planning and execution functions per the acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface geometry data from the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and classify static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent work-zone boundaries and operational areas within the worksite model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Integrate terrain, obstacle, and work-zone data into a unified spatial model accessible to planning and execution functions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data via multi-modal sensing (vision, range, positioning) and store in a unified geometric representation (point clouds, meshes, or voxel grids) with feature classification metadata; expose via standardized query interface.

DP2: Decompose worksite into logical zones (safety perimeters, operational areas, hazard regions) and represent each zone as a parameterized geometric primitive (polygon, polyhedron) with associated feature attributes; maintain zone registry and spatial index.

DP3: Ingest external site documentation (blueprints, CAD models, safety plans) as reference geometry; fuse with real-time sensor observations via registration and alignment algorithms; maintain dual-source provenance.

DP4: Implement a graph-based spatial model where nodes represent discrete site features (obstacles, landmarks, access points) and edges encode spatial relationships (adjacency, containment, line-of-sight); support incremental updates and versioning.
DP5: Establish a time-stamped event log of feature observations and model state changes; enable temporal queries and rollback to support planning under changing site conditions.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP2 + DP4 (hybrid): Represent worksite as a dual-layer model: (1) a zone-based logical decomposition aligned with safety and operational policies (DP2), and (2) a feature graph capturing discrete entities and spatial relationships (DP4). Both layers share a common coordinate frame and are queryable independently; fusion occurs at the interface layer through a spatial index. This supports both policy-driven planning (zones) and fine-grained obstacle avoidance (feature graph).

potential_sources: Zone definitions and feature graph must share consistent coordinate frame and units—mitigated by explicit frame definition and validation at ingestion.
Updates to one layer (e.g., new obstacle detected) must propagate to spatial index—mitigated by event-driven invalidation and lazy re-indexing.
Safety policy constraints may impose hard boundaries on zone geometry—mitigated by treating policies as constraints on DP2 parameterization, not on DP4 feature discovery.
Temporal consistency: if zones are static but features change, model staleness risk—mitigated by DP5 (time-stamped log) to support versioning and rollback.
coupling_check.notes: Hybrid approach decouples logical policy representation (zones) from physical feature discovery (graph), reducing coupling. Both layers are independently queryable and can be updated asynchronously. Coordinate frame and spatial index are the critical integration points; these are well-defined and testable.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite geometry is sufficiently stable during a planning cycle (minutes to hours) to justify snapshot-based model generation. RISK: Rapid site changes (e.g., equipment repositioning) may invalidate model before execution; mitigation: implement change detection and model refresh triggers.

ASSUMPTION: Safety plan and operational policies are formally specified or can be encoded as zone constraints. RISK: Ambiguous or conflicting policies may lead to inconsistent zone definitions; mitigation: require explicit policy formalization and validation before model generation.

ASSUMPTION: Sensor data (vision, range, positioning) is available and has known accuracy/latency characteristics. RISK: Sensor failures or degradation may produce incomplete or corrupted feature data; mitigation: implement sensor health monitoring and graceful degradation (e.g., fall back to prior model state).

ASSUMPTION: External site documentation (blueprints, CAD) is available and reasonably up-to-date. RISK: Stale or inaccurate reference geometry may mislead fusion; mitigation: implement registration confidence scoring and require manual validation for high-impact features.

ASSUMPTION: Planning and execution functions can tolerate model latency of O(seconds) to O(minutes). RISK: If real-time responsiveness is required, incremental update and streaming query support may be needed; mitigation: clarify latency SLA and implement caching/prediction if needed.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite features are discrete and classifiable (obstacles, landmarks, access points). RISK: Ambiguous or continuous features (e.g., soft terrain, dust clouds) may not fit graph model; mitigation: define feature taxonomy explicitly and handle edge cases via metadata flags.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) required feature taxonomy (e.g., static obstacles, dynamic equipment, hazard zones, access points); (2) model update frequency and latency SLA; (3) coordinate frame and accuracy requirements; (4) policy formalization method (e.g., zone parameterization schema). This will enable tighter DP specification and clearer IM boundaries. Additionally, introduce a secondary FR for 'Model Validation and Confidence Reporting' to ensure planning functions can assess model quality and make risk-aware decisions.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized worksite representation and spatial reasoning layer that maintains dual-layer model (zone-based logical decomposition + feature graph) with shared coordinate frame and spatial indexing for policy-driven planning and obstacle avoidance queries

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives real-time vehicle pose, sensor data (LiDAR, camera), and environmental observations to update feature graph and zone occupancy state

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outputs zone-based mission directives and waypoint sequences derived from policy-driven planning layer; receives task requests and constraints

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits safety violations detected via feature graph (obstacle proximity, zone boundary breaches) and policy conflicts from zone-based layer

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Synchronizes dual-layer model state with remote operators and other system nodes; receives updates to zone definitions and safety policies

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: NGHE Solution is the natural home for worksite representation, spatial reasoning, and mission planning. Both zone-based (policy) and feature-graph (obstacle) layers are core to autonomous navigation and guidance. Spatial indexing and coordinate frame management are internal to this module. Minor diffusion: Telemetry ingestion and SafetyAlert emission require external interfaces, but logic remains contained.

Poor fit: Vehicle module should focus on motion control, actuators, and low-level state. Dual-layer worksite model is a planning/reasoning artifact, not a vehicle-specific concern. High diffusion: zone queries and feature graph updates would leak into vehicle control logic; policy enforcement would be misplaced.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a human-machine interface layer for remote control and visualization. While it may consume worksite representation for display, it should not own the dual-layer model or spatial reasoning. High diffusion: policy-driven planning and obstacle avoidance logic would be scattered across UI and backend.

Moderate fit: Safety Services could own policy enforcement and zone-based safety rules, but the feature graph (discrete entities, spatial relationships) is a broader worksite representation concern, not purely safety-focused. Partial diffusion: zone-based logic fits well; feature graph and spatial indexing would need to diffuse to NGHE Solution or a shared layer.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The dual-layer worksite model (zones + feature graph) is a core navigation and planning abstraction that belongs in the NGHE Solution module. This module is responsible for autonomous guidance, mission planning, and spatial reasoning—all of which depend on accurate, queryable worksite representation. The zone-based layer supports policy-driven planning; the feature graph supports fine-grained obstacle avoidance. Both are navigation concerns, not vehicle control, teleoperation UI, or isolated safety logic.
containment_risks: Risk: If Safety Services is given ownership of zone-based policies, there is a risk of duplicating or fragmenting the zone model. Mitigation: Safety Services should consume zone definitions and policy rules from NGHE Solution via a well-defined interface (e.g., SafetyAlert), not own the model itself.

Risk: Teleoperation may need to visualize or query the worksite model for operator situational awareness. Mitigation: Teleoperation consumes read-only snapshots of the dual-layer model via Telemetry or a dedicated visualization interface; it does not modify or own the model.

Risk: Real-time performance of spatial indexing and zone queries could become a bottleneck if NGHE Solution is overloaded. Mitigation: Implement efficient spatial data structures (e.g., R-tree, quadtree) and consider caching zone queries; monitor latency and profile as needed.

Risk: Coordinate frame synchronization across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution could drift if not carefully managed. Mitigation: Establish a single authoritative coordinate frame in NGHE Solution; all external modules reference it via Telemetry or Network Link.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. The selected DP (dual-layer worksite model) is well-suited to NGHE Solution and achieves strong containment. However, clarify the boundary between NGHE Solution and Safety Services: Safety Services should be a consumer of zone-based policies and safety rules (via SafetyAlert and policy configuration interfaces), not an owner of the worksite model. This ensures the DP remains cohesive in NGHE Solution while allowing Safety Services to enforce policies without duplicating spatial reasoning logic.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR ('Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model') is directly verifiable against acceptance criteria: (1) worksite model generation and availability to planning/execution functions, and (2) integration with safety plan and operational policies. The dual-layer DP (zones + feature graph) maps cleanly to these criteria. NGHE Solution as IM provides a single, auditable ownership boundary where model generation, coordinate frame management, and query interfaces can be tested and validated.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is the unambiguous owner of the dual-layer worksite representation. The FR is allocated to a single module with well-defined external interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking/SafetyAlert out, Network Link sync). This eliminates ambiguity about where the model lives and who is responsible for its correctness and performance.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Zone-based policy enforcement could diffuse into Safety Services if ownership boundaries are not explicitly clarified. Mitigation: Safety Services must consume zone definitions and policy rules from NGHE Solution via SafetyAlert and policy configuration interfaces; it does not own or modify the zone model.

Risk: Feature graph updates and spatial indexing queries could leak into Vehicle module if real-time obstacle avoidance is tightly coupled to motion control. Mitigation: Vehicle module receives waypoint sequences and safety alerts from NGHE Solution; it does not directly query or update the feature graph.

Risk: Teleoperation may attempt to own or cache local copies of the worksite model for visualization, leading to state divergence. Mitigation: Teleoperation consumes read-only snapshots via Telemetry or a dedicated visualization interface; all authoritative updates flow through NGHE Solution.

Risk: Coordinate frame synchronization across distributed nodes (Vehicle, Teleoperation, remote operators) could drift if not anchored to a single source of truth. Mitigation: NGHE Solution maintains the authoritative coordinate frame; all external modules reference it via Telemetry or Network Link with explicit frame identifiers.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). The dual-layer worksite model is cohesively owned by NGHE Solution. Both the zone-based logical decomposition (DP2) and feature graph (DP4) are navigation and planning abstractions that belong together in a single module. Spatial indexing, coordinate frame management, and query interfaces are internal to NGHE Solution. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and do not leak core logic. Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable: Telemetry ingestion and SafetyAlert emission are necessary external touchpoints, not logic leakage. The recommended clarification of Safety Services as a consumer (not owner) of zone policies further reduces diffusion risk.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The selected DP (hybrid dual-layer model: zone-based logical decomposition + feature graph with shared coordinate frame and spatial indexing) directly and sufficiently addresses the FR. The DP satisfies both acceptance criteria: (1) a machine-readable worksite model is generated and available to planning/execution functions (via Tasking and Telemetry interfaces), and (2) the model integrates worksite safety plan and operational policies (via zone-based layer and SafetyAlert). The dual-layer approach is justified: zones support policy-driven planning; feature graph supports fine-grained obstacle avoidance. The shared coordinate frame and spatial index enable independent queryability and fusion at the interface layer, avoiding monolithic coupling. No alternative DP is necessary.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly document the boundary between NGHE Solution and Safety Services: Safety Services is a consumer of zone-based policies and safety rules (via SafetyAlert and policy configuration interfaces), not an owner of the worksite model. This prevents duplication and ensures the DP remains cohesive.

Define the Telemetry interface contract in detail: specify the schema for vehicle pose, sensor data (LiDAR, camera), and environmental observations; clarify how these inputs update the feature graph and zone occupancy state in real-time.

Establish a coordinate frame authority protocol: NGHE Solution maintains the authoritative coordinate frame (e.g., UTM, local ENU); all external modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, remote operators) reference this frame via explicit frame identifiers in Telemetry and Network Link messages.

Implement spatial indexing performance requirements: define latency budgets for zone queries and feature graph lookups; specify data structures (e.g., R-tree, quadtree) and caching strategies to meet real-time planning constraints.

Clarify the SafetyAlert interface: specify which safety violations (obstacle proximity, zone boundary breaches, policy conflicts) trigger alerts; define alert severity levels and routing to Safety Services and Teleoperation.

Document the Network Link synchronization protocol: specify how dual-layer model state is synchronized with remote operators and other system nodes; define conflict resolution for concurrent updates to zone definitions and safety policies.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, DP-IM, FR-DP) pass with high confidence and excellent containment (0.92). The dual-layer worksite model DP is well-justified and directly addresses the FR. However, the high safety criticality of the system and the complexity of the DP (zone-based logical decomposition + feature graph + shared coordinate frame + spatial indexing) warrant decomposition into sub-FRs to ensure that critical safety-relevant functions are explicitly specified, verified, and owned. The recommended revisions identify several sub-system concerns (Telemetry interface contract, coordinate frame authority, spatial indexing performance, SafetyAlert routing, Network Link synchronization) that are best addressed through structured sub-FR derivation rather than ad-hoc documentation. This approach maintains the cohesive DP ownership while making safety-critical interfaces and performance requirements explicit and traceable.
next_level_sub_frs: FR 2.1: Ingest and fuse real-time telemetry (vehicle pose, sensor observations) into the worksite feature graph with bounded latency and coordinate frame alignment.
FR 2.2: Maintain authoritative coordinate frame (e.g., UTM, local ENU) and provide frame transformation services to all external modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, remote operators).

FR 2.3: Evaluate zone-based safety policies and operational constraints against current vehicle state and worksite model; emit safety alerts for violations (obstacle proximity, zone boundary breaches, policy conflicts).
FR 2.4: Support real-time spatial queries (zone membership, feature proximity, path feasibility) with latency budgets compatible with planning and obstacle avoidance cycles.

FR 2.5: Synchronize dual-layer worksite model state (zones, feature graph, policies) with remote operators and distributed system nodes via Network Link; resolve concurrent updates and maintain consistency.

FR 2.6: Provide read-only visualization snapshots of the worksite model to Teleoperation and remote operators without exposing authoritative model state or enabling local caching that diverges from NGHE Solution.

revise.why: No revisions to FR, DP, or IM are required at this level. The gate results confirm that the current DP is sound and the allocation to NGHE Solution is clear. Revisions recommended in the gate output (e.g., explicit Safety Services boundary, Telemetry interface contract, coordinate frame authority protocol) are best addressed through sub-FR specification and detailed design, not by reworking the current level.

assumptions: NGHE Solution is the single authoritative owner of the worksite model (zones, feature graph, coordinate frame, spatial index); no other module owns or modifies this state.
Safety Services consumes zone-based policies and safety rules from NGHE Solution via SafetyAlert and policy configuration interfaces; it does not own the worksite model.
Vehicle module receives waypoint sequences and safety alerts from NGHE Solution; it does not directly query or update the feature graph.
Teleoperation and remote operators consume read-only snapshots of the worksite model via Telemetry or a dedicated visualization interface; they do not cache or modify the authoritative model.
Coordinate frame synchronization is anchored to NGHE Solution as the single source of truth; all external modules reference this frame via explicit frame identifiers.
Real-time planning and obstacle avoidance cycles have defined latency budgets (e.g., zone queries < 50 ms, feature graph lookups < 100 ms) that spatial indexing must support.
Telemetry ingestion (vehicle pose, sensor data) and SafetyAlert emission are necessary external touchpoints and do not constitute logic diffusion.
open_risks: Telemetry interface schema and update frequency are not yet specified; mismatch between sensor data rates and feature graph update cycles could cause stale or inconsistent state.

Coordinate frame transformation performance and accuracy under distributed operation (Vehicle, Teleoperation, remote operators) are not yet quantified; frame drift could accumulate and cause safety violations.
SafetyAlert routing and severity levels are not yet defined; unclear which violations trigger alerts to Safety Services vs. Teleoperation vs. both, risking missed or redundant alerts.

Network Link synchronization protocol for concurrent updates to zone definitions and safety policies is not yet specified; conflict resolution strategy could lead to state divergence or policy inconsistency.
Spatial indexing data structures (R-tree, quadtree, etc.) and caching strategies are not yet selected; performance under high-frequency telemetry and large feature graphs is uncertain.
Visualization snapshot interface contract is not yet defined; risk of Teleoperation caching stale snapshots and diverging from authoritative model state.
Boundary between NGHE Solution and Safety Services regarding zone-based policy enforcement is clarified in recommendations but not yet formally documented in design artifacts.
evidence_needed: Detailed Telemetry interface specification: schema for vehicle pose (position, orientation, uncertainty), sensor data (LiDAR point clouds, camera frames, IMU), and environmental observations; update frequency and latency requirements.
Coordinate frame authority protocol: definition of authoritative frame (UTM, local ENU, or other); frame transformation service interface; accuracy and drift tolerance under distributed operation.

SafetyAlert interface specification: enumeration of safety violations (obstacle proximity thresholds, zone boundary breach conditions, policy conflict rules); alert severity levels (critical, warning, info); routing rules to Safety Services, Teleoperation, and Vehicle.

Spatial indexing performance analysis: latency budgets for zone membership queries, feature proximity queries, and path feasibility checks; selection of data structures (R-tree, quadtree, grid-based) and caching strategies; validation against planning and obstacle avoidance cycle times.

Network Link synchronization protocol: specification of dual-layer model state representation (zones, feature graph, policies); conflict resolution strategy for concurrent updates; consistency guarantees (eventual, strong, causal).
Visualization snapshot interface contract: schema for read-only model snapshots; update frequency and latency; mechanisms to prevent local caching and divergence.

Safety Services boundary documentation: explicit statement that Safety Services is a consumer of zone-based policies and safety rules, not an owner of the worksite model; interface contracts for policy configuration and alert consumption.

Integration test plan: verification that Telemetry ingestion, coordinate frame synchronization, spatial queries, SafetyAlert emission, and Network Link synchronization operate correctly under nominal and failure scenarios; validation of latency budgets and consistency guarantees.
